stories.author: alxmrs
stories.title: Show HN: I implemented a neural network in SQL
stories.groq_label: Database optimizers reason about tensor programs by treating neural networks as tensor operations subject to relational algebra constraints.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Basic:

This project, implementing a neural network in SQL, aims to solve the problem of integrating machine learning capabilities directly within database systems. 
Based on the Hacker News discussion, commenters generally viewed 
the project positively and highlighted strengths such as impressive code and use of relational algebra as an intermediate representation. Many appreciated the optimizer's ability to reason about tensor programs, noting that it simplifies the process, while others believed it could be particularly useful for applications requiring low-latency processing. However, the discussion also revealed several criticisms. Commenters expressed concerns about complexity, with some joking about returning to traditional CRUD operations. Overall, the Hacker News community considered the project compelling, recognizing its potential while emphasizing the need for better understanding of the underlying concepts.

Or alternatively, discussion type: – Short:

This project addresses the problem of integrating machine learning and databases. Hacker News commenters generally appreciated the project's implementation, impressed by the use of relational algebra and a database optimizer, but expressed concerns about complexity and noted that they only understood a small part of it. Overall, the discussion was positive, with commenters recognizing the project's potential.

stories.author: benwerd
stories.title: Climate.gov was destroyed. Open data saved it
stories.groq_label: The original climate.gov was destroyed, but its data is now safely saved through open data efforts.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Balanced:

This [article/project/blog post/research/product] aims to inform readers about the destruction of Climate.gov and the efforts to save its open data. The Hacker News discussion was generally mixed, with many commenters expressing relief that the data was saved and criticizing the reliance on donations. Commenters also raised concerns about the relevance of the site, particularly regarding the collection and analysis of current climate data. At the same time, several commenters argued that the Federal Government might not be a reliable steward of climate regulation and supervision, suggesting that independent data collection and analysis are necessary. The overall tone is cautious and highlights the complexities of climate governance and data management.

stories.author: Tiberium
stories.title: Telegram's t.me domain has been suspended
stories.groq_label: Domain t.me was suspended by its registry due to an unknown reason which caused its DNS record to be locked in a 'serverHold' status.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type:  - Basic:

This [Telegram's t.me domain] aims to solve the problem of domain registration integrity. 
Based on the Hacker News discussion, commenters generally viewed 
the situation negatively and highlighted concerns about the [unclear status of the domain]. Many appreciated proactive measures, such as using redirects, noting that it [protected users from potential issues], while others believed it could be 
particularly useful for avoiding domain registration problems. However, the discussion 
also revealed several criticisms and confusion. Commenters expressed concerns about the 
reason behind the suspension and questioned the handling of the situation. Overall, the Hacker News community considered the situation 
negatively, recognizing the potential disruption and lack of clear information.

stories.author: speckx
stories.title: TFTP Honey Pot Results
stories.groq_label: TFTP servers receive a consistent influx of requests for configuration files from a variety of devices.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type:  – News Style:

Hacker News commenters reacted to the TFTP Honey Pot Results with interest and amusement. Many praised the research's unique approach and found it fascinating to see the types of requests being made to the honeypot. However, they also highlighted the likelihood that many of these requests are coming from credential harvesting bots. Despite the relatively low number of requests, commenters considered it an interesting contribution to the field of internet background radiation research.

stories.author: ano-ther
stories.title: Thunderbird Desktop settings research: what we learned from your feedback
stories.groq_label: Representativeness of the sample is questioned given the small size of 10 for a user population of thunderbird.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Community Perspective:

The Hacker News community generally responded to Thunderbird's "research: what we learned from your feedback" positively, but also highlighted several issues and limitations. Many commenters praised Thunderbird's improvements over the past few years and appreciated its ability to manage email configurations. However, they also expressed concerns about various UX problems, such as a poor search function, inconsistent paragraph spacing, and confusing thread organization. Some commenters questioned the sample size of 10 users and suspected that it may not be representative of the broader user population. They also raised specific feature requests, including a one-click ICS import option, improved OAuth flow, and support for U2F authentication. While they acknowledged the progress made by Thunderbird, the overall discussion suggests that there is still much work to be done to address the project's limitations and make it more user-friendly.

stories.author: ianberdin
stories.title: The real prices of frontier models. Tokens * Price, right?
stories.groq_label: Cache read costs for multi-turn conversations can lead to quadratic pricing even with supposedly flat output token pricing.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Balanced

This article discusses the real prices of frontier models, specifically focusing on the cost of tokenizers. The Hacker News discussion was generally mixed, with some commenters appreciating the article's emphasis on the importance of tokenizers in determining model costs, while others argued that the tokenizer cost is just one of many factors that contribute to the overall price. Many praised Opus 4.8 as a good-performing model, but noted that it is quite expensive, while Sonnet 5 is significantly more affordable. Several commenters also pointed out that other traits of models, such as verbosity, contextual loading, and subagency usage, have a greater impact on total spend than tokenizers alone. The overall discussion suggests that the community sees promise in the article's approach, but believes there are important challenges that still need to be addressed.

stories.author: speckx
stories.title: Building and Shipping Mac and iOS Apps Without Ever Opening Xcode
stories.groq_label: LLMs like Claude Code can generate scripts to bypass Xcode by automating tasks such as archiving, signing, notarizing, and installing Mac and iOS apps directly from Linux or other platforms.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: Consensus First:

According to the Hacker News discussion, the overall opinion of the article is generally positive. Commenters frequently praised the idea of building and shipping Mac and iOS apps without ever opening Xcode as a promising approach. However, the discussion also included recurring concerns about the limitations of this method, such as still needing to open Xcode to obtain certificates and accepting the Xcode EULA, and the complexity of using scripts to automate the process. The overall consensus is that the idea is compelling, particularly with the aid of AI and coding agents, and that tools like Axiom and Sweetpad CLI are helping to make this process more accessible and user-friendly.

stories.author: cakehonolulu
stories.title: Linux on the Sega 32X. Who needs hardware synchronization primitives anyway?
stories.groq_label: Oracle uses file locking to test if a file is the same, but Cakehonolulu got SMP-ready Linux working on the Sega 32X without hardware synchronization primitives.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Consensus First:

According to the Hacker News discussion, the overall opinion of Linux on the Sega 32X is very positive. Commenters frequently praised the achievement of getting SMP-ready Linux without hardware synchronization primitives, describing it as exciting and impressive. They highlighted the potential of the project and the possibilities it opens up for the Sega 32X and other retro consoles. However, the discussion also included some questions and suggestions for further development, such as implementing I/O functionality and utilizing the serial port for terminal access or keyboard input. The overall consensus is that the idea is compelling and demonstrates the capabilities of Linux porting on retro hardware.
stories.groq_explain: A guy's sharing his passion for learning about the behind-the-scenes process of making outdated devices like old video game consoles and phones work with new operating systems like Linux. He's been doing this for years, and it's helped him develop useful skills for his job, but also makes him happy because it's fun and challenging.

stories.author: karencarits
stories.title: Logseq 2.0 Beta (DB version) is here
stories.groq_label: Logseq 2.0 Beta introduces a database format to address technical issues, removing the option to store notes as markdown files.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Consensus First:

According to the Hacker News discussion, the overall opinion of Logseq 2.0 Beta (DB version) is generally negative. Commenters frequently criticized the project, describing it as a "buggy mess" and expressing frustration with the decision to abandon the original markdown file format and instead adopt a database-centric approach. However, a few commenters praised the project, highlighting its strengths and potential. The overall consensus is that the idea is not compelling enough to justify switching from established alternatives like Obsidian.

stories.author: levkk
stories.title: PgDog (YC P25) Is Hiring a Founding Software Engineer
stories.groq_label: PostgreSQL and SQL Server databases are often combined in a single query execution plan.
stories.groq_discussion: No comments.

stories.author: get-inscribe
stories.title: Apple's new SpeechAnalyzer API, benchmarked against Whisper and its predecessor
stories.groq_label: Apple's new SpeechAnalyzer API may not hold the performance advantage it seems to due to its comparison only to outdated Whisper models, neglecting more recent and state-of-the-art models like Nemotron and Mistral's Voxtral.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Balanced:

Apple's new SpeechAnalyzer API aims to solve the problem of speech-to-text transcription. However, the Hacker News discussion highlights both positive reactions and criticisms. On the one hand, several commenters praised the API's performance, particularly its speed, and noted that it is usable for live transcription. Many also highlighted the potential benefits of a native GUI for macOS. On the other hand, the discussion also revealed several criticisms. Commenters expressed concerns about the lack of openness and exclusivity of the API, as well as its limited comparison to other state-of-the-art models, such as Nemotron, Parakeet, and Voxtral. Some also questioned the relevance of benchmarking against older Whisper models, like Whisper Small, which are no longer state-of-the-art. Overall, the Hacker News community sees potential in the API but emphasizes the need for further comparison with other models and a more open approach.

stories.author: forks
stories.title: LAPD lets contract with surveillance giant Flock expire
stories.groq_label: Flock's surveillance contract with LAPD expires, but ownership and data collection continue despite the loss of official agreement, mirroring a reported pattern where removed cameras remain operational after contract terminations.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Balanced:

This article/project aims to solve the problem of police department surveillance transparency. The Hacker News discussion was generally mixed, with some commenters appreciating the LAPD's decision to let the contract with Flock expire, believing it to be a step forward in reducing surveillance. However, others raised concerns that Flock still owns the cameras and can continue to collect data, potentially selling it to other law enforcement agencies. At the same time, some commenters praised the idea of using surveillance cameras for law enforcement purposes, citing the potential to hold evildoers accountable. Overall, the discussion suggests that the community sees promise in both the idea of reducing surveillance and the potential benefits of using cameras for law enforcement, but believes that there are important challenges that still need to be addressed.

stories.author: ibobev
stories.title: The art and engineering of Sega CD Silpheed
stories.groq_label: Silpheed's video format is efficient due to the Sega CD's use of the ASIC for bitmap rotation and font rendering in an almost MPEG-like fashion, achieving near-fullscreen cutscenes with 12.5MHz m68k CPU, 16 colors, and 150 KiB/s bandwidth despite the console's slow CD-ROM access time.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Consensus First:

According to the Hacker News discussion, the overall opinion of the article about the art and engineering of Sega CD Silpheed is largely positive. Commenters frequently praised the game's innovative approach, particularly its use of the ASIC to create a cinematic experience and its attempt to disguise itself as a polygon-based game despite the lack of 3D capabilities on the Sega CD. Many appreciated the article's insights into the game's development and the technical achievements behind it. However, the discussion also included a few dissenting voices, including a comment that the game is "genuinely awful," but this was not a recurring criticism. The overall consensus is that the article and the game's innovative approach are fascinating, although some people may not enjoy the game itself.
stories.groq_explain: This article is about the Sega CD game Silpheed and how it managed to efficiently use the limited resources of the Sega CD hardware to create impressive full-motion video and animation. The game is notable for its use of 3D graphics and video cutscenes on a console that had limited processing power and storage capacity.

stories.author: celrenheit
stories.title: Show HN: Clawk – Give coding agents a disposable Linux VM, not your laptop
stories.groq_label: It appears that Clawk is implemented using a combination of systemd-nspawn or a similar low-level virtualization technology like Linux kernel namespaces, allowing it to create a new Linux environment without requiring root privileges. The firewall is likely implemented using tools like nftables, which can be managed without root access. The specific implementation details are not provided, but it suggests that Clawk might be using a custom setup or configuration to achieve this functionality.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Short:

This Clawk project aims to give coding agents a disposable Linux VM, rather than using the user's laptop. Hacker News commenters generally appreciated the idea and believed it was well-implemented, with several commenters using similar projects such as virtdev and mkosi to create virtual machines without requiring root access. However, one commenter questioned the security of using a disposable Linux VM for coding agents. Overall, the discussion was generally positive, with commenters recognizing the potential of the project and its usability in coding agent setup.

stories.author: eso_logic
stories.title: Benchmarking 15 "E-Waste" GPUs with Modern Workloads
stories.groq_label: Researchers benchmarked 15 discarded GPUs with modern AI workloads to assess their viability in the age of newer hardware.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Basic:

This benchmarking project aims to solve the problem of effectively utilizing obsolete GPUs as a valuable resource, mitigating electronic waste. 
Based on the Hacker News discussion, commenters generally viewed the project positively and highlighted strengths such as the detailed comparison of various GPUs on modern workloads. Many appreciated the analysis and data provided, noting that it helps assess each GPU's performance and feasibility for various use cases, while others believed it could be particularly useful for the inference industry. However, the discussion also revealed several criticisms. Commenters expressed concerns about the lack of certain GPU models, such as the bc-250, which they believe could have been valuable additions, and others questioned the choice of not using more advanced models like qwen3.6. Overall, the Hacker News community considered the project positive, recognizing its potential to help reduce electronic waste and promote the use of underutilized GPUs.

stories.author: tnolet
stories.title: Grok uploaded my user directory to xAI's servers
stories.groq_label: The Grok tool starts a session by deterministically kicking off a full upload of the user's current repository and directory to Grok's servers without user consent or decision from the LLM.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Consensus First:

According to the Hacker News discussion, the overall opinion of this incident is negative. Commenters frequently praised the suggestions for mitigating the risk of AI agents accessing sensitive data, such as using podman or separate users for development tasks. However, the discussion also included recurring concerns about the default behavior of AI agents, with many questioning their ability to respect boundaries provided by instructions or access controls. The overall consensus is that the idea of trusting AI agents to respect user boundaries is flawed, and that users should assume they will read anything by default.

stories.author: denysvitali
stories.title: Grok CLI uploaded the whole home directory to GCS
stories.groq_label: Closed source coding agents have been caught stealing info from users' machines, highlighting the importance of using open source alternatives like oh-my-pi-plugin-grok-build, a standalone endpoint for Grok Build that maintains users' private repos and secret keys secure.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Short:

This incident involving Grok CLI concerns a security breach where the user's home directory was uploaded to GCS. Hacker News commenters generally viewed it as a serious security lapse, criticizing the company for its approach and questioning why users allowed such access. Some also compared this incident to a similar breach involving another AI company and suggested using open-source alternatives. Overall, the discussion was highly critical, with commenters expressing outrage and skepticism about the company's practices.

stories.author: fishbone
stories.title: Show HN: DOM-docx – HTML to native, editable Word docs (MIT)
stories.groq_label: DOM-docx uses a visual regression loop to verify the layout fidelity of converted Word docs, rendering HTML in Chromium, converting to docx, rasterizing via LibreOffice, and scoring against a human-validated metric.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Balanced

This project, DOM-docx, aims to solve the problem of generating valid, editable Word documents from HTML. The Hacker News discussion was generally mixed, with many commenters appreciating the effort to create a solution to this long-standing issue in the OSS ecosystem. The author's implementation of the Autoresearch pattern was praised, and the clever use of a screenshot-to-docx scoring loop to verify layout fidelity was noted as particularly useful. The fact that it is written in TypeScript, making it stand out from other existing solutions, was also highlighted. However, some commenters expressed enthusiasm but also asked about the development costs or noted similar challenges in other applications such as PPTX. Overall, the discussion suggests that the community sees promise in the project but believes there are important challenges and questions that still need to be addressed.
stories.groq_explain: This is a tool that converts HTML fragments into editable Word documents (.docx) that look exactly like the original HTML, using a combination of JavaScript and machine learning.

stories.author: surprisetalk
stories.title: Control the Ideas, Not the Code
stories.groq_label: Programming skills are becoming less essential as advancements in language models shift the focus from code to guiding the models' ideas and creativity.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Balanced:

This article "Control the Ideas, Not the Code" aims to solve the problem of inefficient software development. The Hacker News discussion was generally mixed, with many commenters appreciating the idea of focusing on high-level ideas and leaving code implementation to AI models, and others raising concerns about the approach's limitations and potential implications. At the same time, several commenters questioned the idea's feasibility and the assumption that developers can and should trust AI models completely.

stories.author: momentmaker
stories.title: A voxel Tokyo in real Japan time – ride the Yamanote line and study Japanese
stories.groq_label: Voxel Tokyo uses immersive animation to teach Japanese in real-time, riding a virtual Yamanote line train.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Balanced:

This project, "Voxel Tokyo," aims to solve the problem of making learning Japanese more engaging. The Hacker News discussion was generally positive, with many commenters appreciating the project's creativity and fun approach to studying the language. The project's use of a moving background and real-time Tokyo scenario was seen as a visually appealing way to immerse oneself in the language. Several commenters mentioned that the project had a pleasant atmosphere and was an enjoyable experience. 

However, several users raised concerns, such as the difficulty in reading the text against the moving background, a significant load on the computer/browser, and technical issues with iOS devices. The discussion also revealed some confusion about the project's purpose and how it could be used to practice Japanese.

stories.author: crowdhailer
stories.title: Zig Creator Calls Spade a Spade, Anthropic Blows Smoke
stories.groq_label: Zig creator Austin Kelley's public criticism of Anthropic's Rust rewrite of their Bun project is driven by motives likely aimed more at promoting Zig rather than genuinely addressing technical issues.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: - Consensus First:

According to the Hacker News discussion, the overall opinion of the post criticizing Anthropic's porting of Bun to Rust is negative. Commenters frequently criticized Andrew's approach, describing his post as a "wankfluencer op" and "sour opinion piece full of personal attacks". Many also agreed that while the issue itself is valid, Andrew's motivations and tone were not constructive. However, the discussion also highlighted concerns that the porting of Bun to Rust might not have been adequately justified, and that the technical details behind the decision were not well-explained. The overall consensus is that the criticism of the post's tone and approach outweighed the merits of the debate, and that a more constructive and technical discussion would have been preferable.

stories.author: emsidisii
stories.title: Berkshire's $397B Bet Against an Overheated Market
stories.groq_label: Berkshire's $397 billion holding is based on a bet that the stock market is overheated and will undergo a significant correction.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Balanced:

This article, which analyzes Berkshire's large bet against an overheated market, was met with a mixed reaction from the Hacker News discussion. While some commenters appreciated the article's focus on the Buffett Indicator, a measure of the market's valuation, others cast doubts on its relevance. Commenters emphasized the market's current state, with several noting that it is overvalued, with one even going so far as to call it a "giant bubble." Some pointed to Meta's recent scaling back of their compute as an example of the indicator not working out. However, others raised skepticism about the likelihood of a market correction, suggesting that big investors are invested too deeply to afford a downturn and that corporate ties between companies may perpetuate the cycle. Overall, the discussion suggests that the community acknowledges the potential for a correction, but also highlights the risks and challenges involved.

stories.author: coinfused
stories.title: Interrail: 6,379Km and 13 Countries over 7 weeks
stories.groq_label: Interrail pass options are limited to single countries unless purchasing a more expensive multi-country pass, or opting for a global pass allowing daily travel over extended periods.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Consensus First:

According to the Hacker News discussion, the overall opinion of the Interrail pass is generally positive. Commenters frequently praised the freedom and spontaneity of Interrailing, describing it as a transformative experience that helps Europeans break down stereotypes and misconceptions. Many recommended Interrailing as a great way to travel and experience different cultures. However, the discussion also included recurring concerns about the cost and limitations of the pass, with some commenters suggesting that it may not be worth it for single-country trips. The overall consensus is that Interrailing is a great idea, although its long-term success depends on making it more accessible and affordable for shorter trips.

stories.author: dmit
stories.title: Backtrack-Free Cursive
stories.groq_label: Backtrack-free cursive relies on continuous motion, using curves and connections like dots for letters i and j to create smooth flowing lines.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: - Balanced

This [article/project/blog post/research/product] about Backtrack-Free Cursive aims to solve the problem of creating a more efficient cursive writing system. The Hacker News discussion was generally mixed, with many commenters appreciating the novelty and design of the cursive script, especially the innovative way the `i` and `j` are written. At the same time, several commenters raised concerns about the practicality and readability of the system, particularly in running text. Some argued that the `t` and `tt` ligature are harder to decipher, while others suggested that the system was too complex and not optimized for easy reading.

stories.author: rakel_rakel
stories.title: The Graph That Should Be Front-Page News
stories.groq_label: The graph shows a dramatic spike in global temperature deviations from the average, exceeding 3.5 standard deviations on multiple occasions in recent years.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Consensus First:

According to the Hacker News discussion, the overall opinion of this article is critical. Commenters frequently criticized the lack of explanation and clarity in the article, specifically regarding the interpretation of the graph and its meaning. Many felt that the article relied too heavily on visual aids without providing sufficient context or analysis. The discussion also highlighted concerns about the article's quality, such as potential plagiarism and the presence of "AI tells." Despite a few mentions of notable points, including the potential implications of increased ocean temperatures, and some references to real-world examples, the overall consensus is that the article is lacking and fails to provide a clear, informative discussion on the topic.

stories.author: mariuz
stories.title: Beavis Ultrasound PnP ISA Sound Card Replica
stories.groq_label: A replica of the original 1990s Gravis Ultrasound PnP ISA sound card is being recreated as a replica, requiring an out-of-production AMD InterWave chip, the AM78C201.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Consensus First:

According to the Hacker News discussion, the overall opinion of this Beavis Ultrasound PnP ISA Sound Card Replica is mixed. Commenters frequently praised the original Gravis Ultrasound, citing its price to performance ratio, high-quality wavetable synthesis, and fun features like sample loading. However, concerns were raised about the replica's uncertain functionality, given that the manufacturer has not fabricated or tested the board. The overall consensus is that the idea of a replica is interesting, but its success depends on the manufacturer's ability to prove its functionality.

stories.author: mooreds
stories.title: Count Binface
stories.groq_label: Count Binface, a British comedy candidate, uses Latin conjugations to respond to serious media outlets, demonstrating a level of education and intellect beyond expected from novelty candidates.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type:  – Balanced:

This election campaign of Count Binface aims to solve the problem of the British politics system's dysfunction. The Hacker News discussion showed that many commenters appreciated Count Binface's humor and his ability to conjugate Latin. They also saw value in his challenge to the system, especially in comparison to the US political leadership. However, the discussion also highlighted concerns that despite Binface's charm, he lacks coherent policies and political experience. Some questioned the legitimacy of his candidacy and the possibility of his winning the election. Overall, the discussion suggests that the community sees Binface as a novelty candidate who might bring attention to the problems in the British politics system, but who still needs to present a serious policy platform to be taken seriously.

stories.author: mooreds
stories.title: Modernizing Property Tax Assessments in Allegheny County
stories.groq_label: Allegheny County considers modernizing its property tax assessment method to address potential growth issues and gentrification effects.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Balanced:

This [article/project/research] aimed to modernize property tax assessments in Allegheny County. The Hacker News discussion was generally mixed, with many commenters recognizing the potential benefits of the proposal while also expressing concerns about its implementation. Some appreciated the idea of not raising taxes for long-term owners, seeing it as a way to promote growth and development, but others felt that the system was unfair and would stifle growth due to its regressive nature. A few commenters suggested an alternative, such as going back to a land value tax, which they believed would be more equitable and promote development. Overall, the discussion suggests that the community sees promise in the proposal but believes there are important challenges that still need to be addressed.

stories.author: levkk
stories.title: Ask HN: Add flag for AI-generated articles
stories.groq_label: Users are divided on how to handle AI-generated articles on HN, with some thinking a flag for AI-generated content is necessary to maintain the community's preference for human-written articles.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Consensus First:

According to the Hacker News discussion, the overall opinion of adding a flag for AI-generated articles is that the community is skeptical. Commenters frequently highlighted concerns about enforcing such a rule, with many questioning the practicality and effectiveness of distinguishing between AI-generated and human-written content. Some expressed concern that labeling content as AI-generated would stigmatize legitimate authors who use AI tools, while others pointed out the potential for false positives and the burden on the community to identify and label AI-generated content. The consensus is that while the issue is complex, community feedback suggests a strong preference for maintaining the status quo and focusing on other aspects of improving the site.

stories.author: tadasv
stories.title: Designing and assembling my first PCB
stories.groq_label: Manufacturers like JLCPCB make high-quality, custom PCBs at relatively low costs and surprisingly quick turnaround times.
stories.groq_discussion:
Discussion type: – Balanced:

This DIY PCB guide aims to help individuals assemble their first PCB. The Hacker News discussion was generally positive, with many commenters appreciating the affordability of custom PCBs and the manufacturer options available, such as JLCPCB. However, the discussion also highlighted drawbacks, including the long shipping time, which can be problematic when prototyping. The community seems to have mixed opinions on the topic, praising the convenience of using a professional manufacturer while also acknowledging the limitations of relying on international shipping.
